title: Should police chase stolen cars? Two Central NY departments set opposite policies
summary: Syracuse Police Department and Onondaga County Sheriff's Office have adopted opposite policies on pursuing stolen vehicles. SPD implemented a no-chase policy for stolen cars unless connected to violent felonies, citing safety concerns and ineffectiveness at reducing theft rates (1,268 vehicles stolen in 2024, up 12% YoY). OCSO continues pursuing stolen vehicles, creating inconsistent enforcement across jurisdictions. National data shows police pursuits killed over 3,000 people (2017-2022), with majority for minor offenses.

political_signal.description: Syracuse Police Chief Cecile is championing evidence-based pursuit policy reform aligned with national trend toward public safety prioritization over property recovery. Policy demonstrates data-driven decision-making regarding ineffectiveness of chases in reducing theft epidemic.

processing_notes: Article truncated in source; full content analysis unavailable
OCSO is state agency; note intergovernmental coordination gap between city and county
Two recent fatal pursuits (2022, 2023) involving juveniles in stolen vehicles in Syracuse jurisdiction
Kia/Hyundai theft spike may warrant separate investigation tag
Data point: 86% increase in car thefts since 2019 suggests systemic problem beyond pursuit policy
Buffalo Police Department model referenced as alternative approach (inverse pursuit signaling)
